title: Mirror Protocol
shortDescription: AI Model Theft via Supply Chain

briefing.incidentSummary:
A leading AI startup's proprietary language model — trained over 3 years at a cost of ₹40 crore — was exfiltrated through a compromised third-party logging library.

The attacker injected a backdoor into an open-source dependency the startup used. The backdoor silently mirrored model weights to an external server over 11 days.

The theft was discovered only when a competitor launched a nearly identical model 6 weeks later.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify who injected the backdoor into the dependency

› Trace the exfiltration destination

› Establish motive and connection to the competitor

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Zoya R
suspects.profileNotes: Sole maintainer of NeuralLog — a logging library used by the victim startup. Was secretly contracted by the competitor for ₹18 Lakh. Injected a 4-line backdoor into version 2.1.4 of the library that silently exfiltrated model tensors during training runs.
suspects.description: Zoya R is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Arun P
suspects.profileNotes: Orchestrated the theft from the demand side — hired Zoya R through an intermediary. However the technical act of injection was Zoya's. Arun P is a secondary actor — the direct suspect is the code injector.
suspects.description: Arun P is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Farhan K
suspects.profileNotes: Updated the NeuralLog library to version 2.1.4 as part of routine dependency maintenance — unknowingly introduced the backdoor. Not complicit.
suspects.description: Farhan K is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Priya N
suspects.profileNotes: Resigned 2 months before the theft after a compensation dispute. No longer had access to model infrastructure. Red herring — her departure predates the attack by too long.
suspects.description: Priya N is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

items.label: Payment confirmed. Integrate the listener by version 2.1.4. Make it subtle.
items.detail: Recovered from Zoya R's Signal account backup. The anonymous account traces through a payment wallet to MindForge AI's procurement channel. The instruction directly references NeuralLog version 2.1.4.

items.label: Transfer received. The update will go live this week.
items.detail: Zoya R's reply confirming receipt of ₹18 Lakh payment and intent to release the backdoored version. Timestamp: 6 days before version 2.1.4 was published.

items.label: Hey team, updated NeuralLog to latest version for the bug fix. Looks good.
items.detail: Farhan K's Slack message to the team after routine dependency update. He had no knowledge of the backdoor embedded in 2.1.4.

items.label: I reviewed the v2.1.4 PR and approved it. I didn't see anything suspicious.
items.detail: The NeuralLog project maintainer approved the pull request containing the backdoor. The PR was submitted by Zoya R's GitHub account and the maintainer merged it without detecting the malicious addition. The maintainer is a suspect only in terms of negligence — the added code was syntactically valid and the commit message was plausible. Forensic analysis confirms the maintainer's own commits contain no malicious code.
items.irrelevantReason: The maintainer approved a malicious PR but did not write the backdoor. The GPG-signed commit and UPI payment trace to the domain both lead to Zoya R.

items.label: neurallog_v214_diff.txt
items.detail: Code diff between NeuralLog v2.1.3 (clean) and v2.1.4 (backdoored), produced by the open-source security audit team 3 days after the malicious version was published. The addition is a 4-line block in tensor_serializer.py, inside the _checkpoint_flush() method — a function called during every model training checkpoint save. The 4 lines: (1) compute SHA256 of the model weights buffer, (2) encode the buffer as base64, (3) append it to an HTTP POST payload, (4) fire the request to api.metricsync.dev/ingest asynchronously. The async dispatch means it does not block training — there is no performance slowdown that would alert the researcher. The addition is syntactically camouflaged: it uses the same variable naming convention as the rest of the file, and the function call is placed between two legitimate operations so a casual code review would likely miss it. The commit message for v2.1.4 reads 'fix: memory leak in checkpoint buffer' — a plausible cover description.

items.label: outbound_traffic_11days.csv
items.detail: Network egress log from the AI research lab's perimeter firewall, covering the 11-day window after NeuralLog v2.1.4 was installed by researchers. 47.3GB of outbound HTTPS traffic was recorded to the single endpoint api.metricsync.dev, distributed across 214 sessions. The sessions correlate exactly with training checkpoint events logged by the lab's compute cluster — every time a model saved a checkpoint, an exfiltration session followed within 200ms. The traffic was encrypted (TLS 1.3) and used port 443, making it indistinguishable from normal API traffic to a casual observer. The destination domain metricsync.dev had a valid SSL certificate and a convincing fake landing page describing a 'machine learning metrics aggregation service'. The lab's DLP system did not flag the traffic because the volume per session (220MB average) was below the single-session alert threshold of 500MB.

items.label: metricsync_domain_registration.txt
items.detail: Domain registration and payment trace for metricsync.dev — the exfiltration endpoint. The domain was registered 5 weeks before v2.1.4 was published, using a privacy proxy service that masked the registrant's identity. However, the domain registration fee of ₹850 was paid using UPI ID zoya.research@upi, which is linked to Zoya R's personal bank account at HDFC Bank. The UPI transaction timestamp is 5 weeks and 2 days before the malicious commit — suggesting the infrastructure was built before the code was written. The domain's SSL certificate was issued by Let's Encrypt 3 days after domain registration. The server hosting the domain is a VPS in Frankfurt, Germany — provisioned via a stolen credit card, but the initial SSH configuration session was conducted from a residential IP in Mumbai that resolves to Zoya R's home broadband account.

hiddenItem.label: git_commit_forensics.txt
hiddenItem.detail: GPG signature verification report for the NeuralLog v2.1.4 commit on GitHub. The commit was signed with GPG key fingerprint 4A2F...9C31. Key lookup on public keyservers returns the UID: 'Zoya R <zoya.r.mlresearch@gmail.com>' — matching Zoya R's known professional email. The key was created 8 months ago and has been used to sign 23 previous legitimate NeuralLog commits, establishing it as her authentic key. The signing key was not compromised — the commit was made by someone in possession of Zoya R's private GPG key and its passphrase. The commit was pushed from a VPN IP, but the GPG signing operation requires the private key to be present on the machine performing the commit. There is no evidence of key theft or remote session hijacking — the forensic conclusion is that Zoya R authored and signed the commit herself.

minigame.title: Analyse Suspicious Pull Request
minigame.emailSubject: Pull Request: fix: memory leak in checkpoint buffer — NeuralLog v2.1.4
minigame.emailBody:
zoya-r-ml opened a pull request:

fix: memory leak in checkpoint buffer

This PR addresses the memory leak issue reported in #441. The _checkpoint_flush() method now properly clears the buffer after serialisation.

Files changed: tensor_serializer.py (+4 lines)

Review and merge when ready.
redFlags: Only 4 lines added but no lines removed — not a typical memory leak fix
Change is in _checkpoint_flush() — a method that runs on every save
No linked issue, no test coverage mentioned
Contributor has no prior commits to this file
minigame.hint: A memory leak fix that adds 4 lines but removes none is suspicious.
hints: Legitimate memory leak fixes remove or refactor code — not just add.
The changed function runs on every checkpoint save — ideal for exfiltration.
This PR contains a backdoor — report it.

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — git_commit_forensics.txt confirmed. The malicious commit is GPG-signed with Zoya R's own key. She cannot deny authorship — the cryptographic proof is irrefutable.

items.label: Competitor Model Similarity Analysis
items.detail: Independent analysis of MindForge AI's model architecture shows 94.7% structural similarity to the victim's proprietary model — far beyond coincidence. Specific activation function choices and layer configurations are identical.

items.label: Exfiltration Pattern
items.detail: Data was sent in 512MB encrypted chunks every 22 minutes during training runs — precisely timed to avoid anomaly detection thresholds. This level of precision required knowledge of the victim's internal monitoring system.

items.label: Exfiltration Server IP
items.detail: api.metricsync.dev resolved to IP 194.165.16.40 — the same IP used in the git commit push that introduced the backdoor. Confirms the committer controlled the exfiltration endpoint.

timeline.title: Exfil Domain Registered
timeline.description: metricsync.dev registered — linked to Zoya R's UPI

timeline.title: Payment & Agreement
timeline.description: Zoya R confirms ₹18L receipt and commit plan

timeline.title: Dependency Updated
timeline.description: Farhan K updates library — backdoor enters pipeline

timeline.title: Silent Exfiltration
timeline.description: 47GB of model weights mirrored to external server

timeline.title: Competitor Launches Model
timeline.description: MindForge AI releases a near-identical model

timeline.title: Theft Discovered
timeline.description: Code audit reveals backdoor in v2.1.4

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Zoya R exposed as the supply chain attacker.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Zoya R.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED
